chunks.text: Paul R. Gray is Professor Emeritus and Executive Vice Chancellor and Provost Emeritus at UC Berkeley. He can be contacted at pgray@berkeley.edu with office hours by email appointment only. His biography details that he earned B.S., M.S., and Ph.D. degrees in Electrical Engineering from the University of Arizona between 1963 and 1969. After working at Fairchild Semiconductor from 1969 to 1971 on analog integrated circuits, he joined UC Berkeley's faculty in 1971. His research has focused on bipolar and MOS circuit design, electro-thermal interactions, telecommunications circuits, and analog-digital interfaces.
chunks.question: What are Paul Gray's academic degrees and where did he earn them?
chunks.answer: B.S., M.S., Ph.D. from University of Arizona.

chunks.text: Prof. Gray has authored or co-authored over 150 journal articles and conference papers, receiving best paper awards, and four books including the textbook 'Analysis and Design of Analog Integrated Circuits' in its sixth edition. He holds 14 patents. He is a member and former Councillor of the National Academy of Engineering, an IEEE Fellow, and has received numerous awards such as the IEEE Solid-State Circuits Award (1994), National Outstanding Researcher Award (2000), IEEE James H. Mulligan, Jr. Education Medal (2004), IEEE Robert Noyce Medal (2008), and NAE Simon Ramo Founders award (2024). He also has honorary doctorates from the University of Bucharest (1999) and Swiss Federal Institute of Technology in Lausanne (2006).
chunks.question: What major awards has Paul Gray received?
chunks.answer: IEEE Solid-State Circuits Award, NAE Simon Ramo award.

chunks.text: At UC Berkeley, Prof. Gray served as Chairman of the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences (1990-93), Dean of the College of Engineering (1996-2000), and Executive Vice Chancellor and Provost (2000-2006). He was a board member of the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation for 14 years, including as interim President and board chair, and serves on several corporate and nonprofit boards. His research areas include Design, Modeling and Analysis (DMA) and Integrated Circuits (INC).
chunks.question: What administrative roles did Paul Gray hold at UC Berkeley?
chunks.answer: Department Chair, Dean, Executive Vice Chancellor.

chunks.text: Selected publications include 'Analysis and Design of Analog Integrated Circuits' (4th ed., 2001), 'A 1.9-GHz wide-band IF double conversion CMOS receiver for cordless telephone applications' (1997), 'A 10-bit, 20-MS/s, 35-mW pipeline A/D converter' (1994), and 'MOS switched-capacitor filters' (1979). Other works cover MOS technologies, sampled-data ladder filters, and charge redistribution analog-to-digital conversion techniques from the 1970s and 1980s, highlighting his contributions to integrated circuit design and analog electronics.
chunks.question: What is a key textbook authored by Paul Gray?
chunks.answer: Analysis and Design of Analog Integrated Circuits.

chunks.text: Selected honors and awards include the NAE Simon Ramo Founders Award (2024), Silicon Valley Engineering Hall of Fame (2015), IEEE Robert N. Noyce Medal (2008), Berkeley Citation (2006), Benjamin Garver Lamme Award (2005), IEEE James H. Mulligan Jr. Education Medal (2004), and IEEE Donald O. Pederson Award (1994). He became a National Academy of Engineering member in 1990, an IEEE Fellow in 1981, and received the IEEE W. R. G. Baker Prize Paper Award in 1980, among others like the SIA University Research Award (2000) and IEEE Morris N. Liebmann Memorial Award (1983).
chunks.question: When did Paul Gray receive the IEEE Robert N. Noyce Medal?
